title: Asynchronous Tool Execution
concept: Running slow tools in the background and letting the agent check back for results in later turns.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why do we put a timeout on a tool like a database query?

options.text: So it can't hang forever and freeze the agent
To make the query return wrong answers

exercises.explain: A timeout is a strict time limit. If a tool takes too long, the timeout stops it so the agent doesn't get stuck waiting forever.

cards.title: Don't just stand there!
cards.body: Some tasks take a long time, like rendering a video or training a model. If the agent froze and waited, it would waste minutes doing nothing. What if it could start the task and come back later?

cards.title: What is async tool execution?
cards.body: Asynchronous execution means the agent kicks off a slow tool in the background instead of waiting. It gets a ticket, keeps working, and checks back for the result in a later turn.

cards.title: Like a coffee shop buzzer
cards.body: You order a coffee and get a buzzer. Instead of standing at the counter, you sit down and do other things. When the buzzer lights up, you go grab your drink. The agent works the same way with slow tools.

cards.title: In action
cards.body: An agent asks a tool to render a 5-minute video. The tool replies 'Job started, ID #42.' The agent answers other user questions, then later checks 'Is job #42 done?' and gets the finished video.

cards.title: Myth: async means instant
cards.body: Async does NOT make the tool faster. The slow task still takes the same time. Async just frees the agent to do other things while it waits instead of being stuck.

cards.title: Waiting vs. checking back
cards.body: Compare a frozen agent to one that keeps moving.

left.text: Agent freezes and waits for the slow tool to finish before doing anything else.

right.text: Agent starts the tool, keeps working, and checks back for the result later.

exercises.prompt: What does an agent usually get back right away when it starts an async tool?

options.text: A job ticket or ID to check on later

exercises.explain: Async tools hand back a ticket or job ID immediately. The agent uses that ID later to check whether the result is ready.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Asynchronous execution makes a slow tool finish faster.
exercises.explain: False. The task still takes the same amount of time. Async just lets the agent do other work instead of freezing while it waits.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about how the agent gets the result.
exercises.sentence: After starting a slow tool, the agent checks back in a later turn to see if the result is ready.
exercises.explain: With async execution, the agent returns in a later turn to collect the finished result instead of waiting the whole time.

exercises.prompt: Which reply looks AI-generated for an agent starting an async render job?

options.text: Render started successfully. Job ID #42 is now processing in the background; I'll check back for the result shortly.
render go now maybe done idk

exercises.explain: The first reply is clear, complete, and polished, giving a job ID and next step, which is typical of AI-generated text.

exercises.prompt: A user asks an agent to train a model that takes 20 minutes. Why is asynchronous execution the smart choice here?

options.text: The agent starts the job, gets a job ID, answers other questions, then checks back when training is done, so it never freezes for 20 minutes
Async makes the 20-minute training finish in 1 second
The agent should freeze and ignore the user for 20 minutes
The agent should cancel any task longer than 1 minute

exercises.explain: Long jobs like training would waste time if the agent just froze. With async execution, the agent launches the job, receives a job ID, stays useful, and returns later to grab the finished result. Async doesn't speed the task up, it just keeps the agent free and responsive while the work happens in the background.
